critic_raw:
Issue 1: Before-after without concurrent control is weak causal design.
Issue 2: Process metrics not measured — cannot distinguish alert effect from antibiotic protocol effect.
Issue 3: Lactate ordering criterion may select lower-acuity patients mechanically.
defender_raw: Round 1: On Issue 1 — concede. Without concurrent control, the design is insufficient for causal attribution. Prior-year same-season is a feasible alternative. On Issue 2 — concede. Process metrics (time-to-antibiotic, appropriateness) should be measured to distinguish alert effect from protocol effect. On Issue 3 — concede. If lactate ordering expanded to lower-acuity patients, the mortality improvement could be mechanical cohort dilution.

debate_rounds.critic: Full concession. Critique wins. Prior-year same-season comparison, process metric analysis, and lactate ordering distribution analysis are all required before hospital-wide extension is justified.
debate_rounds.defender: Round 1: Full concession. The current analysis is insufficient for the stated causal claim. A controlled evaluation is required.

points_resolved: Issue 2: process metrics not measured — critique_wins
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: weak before-after design without concurrent control
Issue 2: process metrics not measured to distinguish mechanisms
Issue 3: lactate ordering expansion may mechanically reduce observed mortality

empirical_test.condition: Prior-year same-season comparison; process metric analysis; lactate ordering rate and acuity distribution comparison
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Prior-year shows comparable mortality; antibiotic metrics changed with protocol not model; lactate acuity distribution shifted lower
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Prior-year mortality higher than model period; time-to-review correlates with model flag; lactate distribution unchanged
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Some improvement beyond baseline but multiple mechanisms changed simultaneously
